narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, with your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 standing out above the typical student's 3.21, and your LSAT score of 35 reflecting solid preparation. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 56.5% probability that you would fail it—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance compared to peers at that stage. This outweighed the positive push from your Year 1 decile of 5, which was close to the average of 5.71 and helped argue for your potential to pass. Your family income also nudged the outlook slightly toward higher risk, while your stronger-than-average undergrad grades and LSAT score offered some counterbalance, though not enough to shift the overall decision. Full-time status provided a minor favorable influence, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. In essence, the model saw your mid-Year 3 grades as the dominant concern when stacked against typical students who pass the bar, despite your other strengths. We understand this outcome may feel disheartening, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive academic paths. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your later-year performance, as improving that area could dramatically strengthen future applications. You might also explore programs with bar passage support tailored for diverse students like yourself. Keep pushing—your UGPA and LSAT show you have the foundation to succeed with the right adjustments.
